text: Hello dear viewer, welcome to my channel. My name is Dr. Vishal Soni and I would be demonstrating a robotic two-way fundoplication for a hiatus hernia. Greetings from Zydus Hospital and the Hernia Clinic. I would be demonstrating three specific steps in this particular surgery that are of tremendous interest especially to the upper GI surgeons and the surgeons who deal in with this particular surgery.

The surgeon uses a fenestrated bipolar instrument in the left hand and a monopolar cautery scissor in the right hand to sequentially cauterize and divide the vessels encountered near the body and antrum region, working up to the gastroesophageal junction. The laparoscopic curved grasp pump assists in the procedure.

The surgeon isolates and coagulates vessels near the greater curvature of the stomach using a fenestrated bipolar instrument, remaining close to the stomach wall. Occasional bleeding is observed, but hemostasis is achieved effectively. The surgeon continues to divide short gastric vessels using monopolar current and scissors, carefully navigating retro gastric adhesions between the stomach and pancreas or spleen. The assistant manipulates the greater curvature to facilitate vessel definition and division.

The surgeon encounters the last vessels near the GE junction, achieving hemostasis with a fenestrated bipolar before dividing them with scissors. The GE junction is clearly visible. The next step involves crural repair using a braided, permanent 2-0 Ethibond suture. The surgeon employs a figure-of-eight suturing technique, ensuring adequate space is left next to the esophagus to prevent tightness. The fascia over the right and left crural pillars is preserved to prevent muscle fiber separation and potential recurrence.

The surgeon places a stitch anteriorly to narrow the hiatus, ensuring the gastroesophageal junction and lower esophagus are in an anatomical position. A figure of eight suture technique is used, taking care to avoid penetrating the pericardium or pleura. An intraoperative scope is used to assess the diaphragmatic pinch before proceeding with a two-phase fundoplication, wrapping the GE junction with an inch to an inch and a half of stomach tissue.

The surgeon completes the posterior partial two-phase fundoplication, securing the wrap around the GE junction using a 2-0 ethibond suture. The GE junction is visibly isolated and dissected, with the umbilical tape being removed to finalize tissue alignment.
